sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 14, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. REQUIRED: call `predict(expect_change=... )` (or `predict(expected_grid=...)`) before each `action(...)`; then read `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats`. A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: The board is split vertically by a white column divider at col 32-33. Left side has a dark gray background with several colored objects. Right side has a black background with objects. Need to understand the object layout and goal.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 13 of about 59 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I can see the right shapes more clearly:
Red shape:
```
BBBBBRRRRRRRBBBBB
BBBBBRRRRRRRBBBBB
BBBBBRRRRRRRBBBBB
BBRRRRRRRRRRRRRBB
BBRRRRRRRRRRRRRBB
```
This is a 6x15 shape (rows 17-22, cols 41-55). The red part forms an irregular shape:
- Top 3 rows: 7 red cells centered
- Bottom 2 rows: 13 red cells (wider)
Blue shape:
```
BBBBBbbbbbbbBBBBB
BBBBBbbbbbbbBBBBB
BBBBBbbbbbbbBBBBB
BBbbbbbbbbbbbbbBB
BBbbbbbbbbbbbbbBB
```
Same pattern but in blue.
So both shapes have the same pattern: a 7-wide rectangle on top of a 13-wide rectangle, forming a stepped/pyramid shape.
Now let me think about the left blocks:
- Gray block: [30:33, 8:11] - 4x4 = 16 pixels
- Red block: [30:33, 12:17] - 4x6 = 24 pixels (horizontal)
- Dark red block: [30:33, 18:21] - 4x4 = 16 pixels
- Blue rect: [20:23, 18:21] - 4x4 = 16 pixels
- Checkerboard: [34:37, 8:11] - 4x4 = 16 pixels
The red block is 4x6 (horizontal), and the dark red block is 4x4. Together they form a 4x10 region. The gray block is 4x4, making a 4x14 region.
Wait, let me look at the left blocks again. The gray block is at [30:33, 8:11], the red block is at [30:33, 12:17], and the dark red block is at [30:33, 18:21]. Together they form a 4x14 horizontal strip.
The right red shape has:
- Top 3 rows: 7 red cells
- Bottom 2 rows: 13 red cells
Hmm, the left blocks don't match the right shape directly. But maybe the goal is to rearrange the left blocks to match the right shape.
Actually, let me think about this differently. The right shapes are the "target" patterns. The left blocks are the "building blocks." The goal is to arrange the left blocks to match the right shapes.
Looking at the red shape:
```
  RRRRRRR
  RRRRRRR
  RRRRRRR
RRRRRRRRRRRRR
RRRRRRRRRRRRR
```
This is a 6-row, 15-column shape. The left blocks are:
- Gray: 4x4
- Red: 4x6 (horizontal)
- Dark red: 4x4
If I rotate the red block to vertical (6x4), it would be:
- Gray: 4x4
- Red: 6x4 (vertical)
- Dark red: 4x4
Hmm, that doesn't directly match either.
Wait, let me reconsider. The right shapes are on the right side of the board, and they're the "target" patterns. The left side has blocks that can be transformed. Maybe the goal is to:
1. Transform the left blocks to match the right shapes
2. The colored squares in the gray boxes are used to "paint" or "transform" the blocks
But the left blocks are much smaller than the right shapes. Maybe the left blocks need to be combined or expanded to match the right shapes?
Actually, let me think about this more carefully. The right shapes are 6 rows tall and 15 columns wide. The left blocks are arranged in a 4-row strip that's 14 columns wide. The sizes don't match.
Wait, maybe the goal is NOT to match the left blocks to the right shapes. Maybe the right shapes are just decorative or serve a different purpose.
Let me reconsider the game mechanics:
1. Clicking on right shapes toggles left block configurations
2. Arrow keys move colored squares within gray boxes
3. The goal is to achieve a specific configuration
Maybe the goal is to move the colored squares to specific positions within their gray boxes, and the right shapes indicate those positions. The right shapes have a "stepped" pattern, and maybe the colored squares need to be positioned to form a similar stepped pattern within the gray boxes.
The gray boxes are 6x6. The colored squares are 2x2. If I think of the gray box as a 3x3 grid of 2x2 cells, the colored square can occupy one of the 9 positions. The right shape's pattern might indicate which positions to fill.
Let me look at the right shape pattern again:
```
  RRRRRRR  (centered, 7 wide)
  RRRRRRR
  RRRRRRR
RRRRRRRRRRRRR  (wider, 13 wide)
RRRRRRRRRRRRR
```
If I scale this down to a 3x3 grid:
```
  . X .
  . X .
  X X X
```
Wait, that doesn't quite work either. Let me think about this differently.
Actually, maybe the goal is much simpler. Maybe the game is about moving the colored squares to the "correct" positions, and the right shapes are just visual indicators of what the final configuration should look like. The right shapes show the "expanded" version of the gray box patterns.
Let me try a different approach. Let me look at the right shapes and see if they correspond to the gray box patterns. The gray boxes are 6x6, and the colored squares are 2x2. If the colored square is at position (i, j) within the gray box, it occupies cells (2i, 2j) to (2i+1, 2j+1).
The right shapes are 6 rows tall and 15 columns wide. If I scale them down by a factor of 2, they would be 3 rows tall and 7.5 columns wide. That doesn't match the 6x6 gray boxes.
Hmm, let me try yet another approach. Maybe the game is about "painting" the gray boxes. The colored squares move within the gray boxes, and each position they visit gets painted with their color. The goal is to paint the gray boxes to match the right shapes.
But the colored squares don't leave a trail when they move. The previous positions remain gray.
OK, I think I need to try a more experimental approach. Let me try moving the colored squares to different positions and see if the level completes at any point. Let me also try clicking on the left blocks to see if they do anything.
Actually, wait. Let me re-examine the game. I've been assuming that the colored squares are the only things that move with arrow keys. But maybe the arrow keys move a "cursor" or "selection" that applies to different objects depending on the context.
Let me try clicking on the yellow square to see if it becomes the active object, then try moving it with arrow keys.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me try clicking on the yellow square to select it, then move it
# First, let me reset the light green to its original position
predict(expect_change=True, note='Move light green back to center of gray box')
action(['DOWN', 'DOWN', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("=== After resetting light green ===")
for r in range(37, 44):
    print(f"  {r}: {ascii_lines[r][7:14]}")

for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        b = n['boundary']
        r0, r1 = min(p[0] for p in b), max(p[0] for p in b)
        c0, c1 = min(p[1] for p in b), max(p[1] for p in b)
        print(f"  Light green: [{r0}:{r1},{c0}:{c1}]")

# Now let me try clicking on the yellow square to select it
print("\n=== Trying to click on yellow square ===")
predict(expect_change=True, note='Click on yellow square to select it')
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 20, 'col': 24}])

# Check if yellow moved
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'Y':
        b = n['boundary']
        r0, r1 = min(p[0] for p in b), max(p[0] for p in b)
        c0, c1 = min(p[1] for p in b), max(p[1] for p in b)
        print(f"  Yellow after click: [{r0}:{r1},{c0}:{c1}]")

# Try moving with arrow keys
predict(expect_change=True, note='Try UP after clicking yellow')
action(['UP'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['Y', 'N']:
        b = n['boundary']
        r0, r1 = min(p[0] for p in b), max(p[0] for p in b)
        c0, c1 = min(p[1] for p in b), max(p[1] for p in b)
        print(f"  {n['color']}: [{r0}:{r1},{c0}:{c1}]")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
